name: Rattlesnake Mt.
short_description: Rockhound Rattlesnake Mt. for colorful agate and jasper specimens on gently sloping terrain in central Washington's semi-arid landscape.
description:
Rattlesnake Mt. in Benton County offers rockhounders access to scenic mid-elevation basalt terrain where silica-rich mineral deposits yield quality agate, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. The gentle slopes make this site approachable for collectors of mixed experience levels, with parking accessible via standard roads and hiking required to reach productive collecting areas.

The area sits within the Columbia Plateau, where Miocene-age basaltic lava flows and associated rhyolitic deposits created favorable conditions for silica precipitation and secondary mineral formation. Weathering of these volcanic rocks has exposed agate nodules and chalcedony-bearing fracture zones that fracture naturally into collectible pieces.

Best collecting occurs on gentle slopes and wash areas where erosion has concentrated loose specimens. The site offers views across the Hanford Reach area and surrounding shrub-steppe landscape. Elevation at the summit is just under 3000 feet, making this accessible year-round with proper seasonal planning.

Expect moderate terrain with rocky footing and exposed basalt outcrops. Bring plenty of water as the semi-arid climate offers little shade. The site is popular with local rockhounders and offers good value as public access land with minimal facilities.
history: Rattlesnake Mt. was formed during the Miocene epoch when Columbia River basalt floods covered the region, creating the plateau's volcanic foundation. Subsequent folding and erosion of these basalts exposed silica-rich layers where groundwater deposited agate and chalcedony, creating the mineral concentrations collectors find today.
tips: Search the gentle slopes and erosional washes on the mountain's north and east faces where gravity and water have concentrated loose agate and jasper specimens. Bring a 10x hand lens to identify chalcedony-filled fractures in basalt before attempting to extract material. Look for specimens with banded colors - tan, white, and reddish-brown bands indicate quality agate; bring a rubberized deadblow hammer and chisels to carefully extract nodules from host rock. The site receives strong afternoon sun exposure, so start collecting early and allow 3-4 hours for a productive session at higher elevations where mineral density is greatest.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2998 feet with gentle to moderate slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain consists of exposed basalt outcrops, rocky footing, and minimal vegetation - mostly short shrubs typical of semi-arid plateau country. Walking distance from parking to productive areas is 0.5 to 2 miles depending on route chosen. Footing is stable but rocky; sturdy hiking boots are essential. South and west slopes receive significant afternoon heat and sun exposure.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Spring and fall offer moderate temperatures, lower UV intensity, and good access after snow melt or before winter weather. Summers are very hot (regularly exceeding 90 degrees F) with minimal shade; early morning starts essential if collecting June - August. Winter can bring snow and ice at elevation; avoid December - February.
written_directions: From Benton (approximate center of Benton County), head north on WA-240 for approximately 12 miles toward the Hanford Reach area. Continue northeast on local roads toward the Rattlesnake Lake area. GPS coordinates 46.395714, -119.613123 pinpoint the summit access point. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for final approach roads, which may be rough or gated depending on current land management status. Confirm current access conditions with Benton County Parks or local rockhounding clubs before visiting, as this site borders sensitive lands.
road_conditions: Final approach roads unpaved and potentially rough with washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Confirm road status before visiting.
parking_notes: Informal pullout parking at trailhead access point. Limited capacity; arrive early. No developed facilities or amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or blasting. Remove only loose specimens and detached pieces; do not use mechanized tools or alter the landscape.
rules:

No motorized excavation

nearest_services: Benton, WA approximately 12 miles south has gas, food, and water services. Richland, WA approximately 15 miles south has full services.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with unstable footing in places. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Hiking required with elevation gain of 300-500 feet depending on route. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Not suitable for limited mobility. South-facing slopes have minimal shade and intense afternoon sun exposure; heat management critical in warm months.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Rattlesnake Mt. in Washington?
faq.answer: Rattlesnake Mt. is known for quality agate, chalcedony, jasper, and general quartz specimens. The site's Miocene basalt formations and secondary silica deposits produce banded agate with tan, white, and reddish-brown coloring, plus translucent chalcedony pieces ideal for cutting or display. Most specimens range from small thumb-sized pieces to fist-sized nodules.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Rattlesnake Mt. free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Rattlesnake Mt. is completely free public land access. There are no permit fees, entrance fees, or parking charges. You do need to follow BLM regulations including the 25 lb per day collection limit and personal use only requirements. Always confirm current access status with Benton County Parks before visiting.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Rattlesnake Mt., Washington?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding seasons are April through May and September through October when temperatures are moderate (60-75 degrees F) and collecting is most comfortable. Summer months (June-August) are extremely hot with afternoon temperatures exceeding 90 degrees F and minimal shade; if collecting then, start at dawn. Avoid December through February due to potential snow and ice at elevation.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect agates at Rattlesnake Mt. in Benton County?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal rockhounding at Rattlesnake Mt. The site is public BLM land open to casual collecting. However, you must follow all collection rules: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation. Do not remove specimens that are part of living formations - collect only loose pieces and detached nodules.

faq.question: What tools should I bring for rockhounding agate at Rattlesnake Mt.?
faq.answer: Essential tools include a 10x hand lens to identify quality specimens, a deadblow hammer for gently extracting nodules, cold chisels for precision work, and a sturdy rock hammer. Bring heavy gloves to protect against sharp basalt, and pack plenty of water (at least 3 liters) as the semi-arid terrain offers no natural water sources. A hiking daypack, sun protection, and sturdy boots are critical for the rocky, exposed slopes.

faq.question: Is Rattlesnake Mt. good for beginner rockhounders in Washington?
faq.answer: Yes, Rattlesnake Mt. is suitable for rockhounders ages 8 and up with moderate experience. The gentle slopes and abundant loose specimens make it less technical than expert sites, but the rocky footing, significant heat exposure, and 1-2 mile hikes require reasonable fitness. Families should plan for early morning starts in warmer months, bring extra water, and supervise children closely around unstable terrain and abandoned mining features that may exist in the area.
